{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} | image = Tatsuta-Taisha03.JPG | caption = [[Torii]] at the entrance of Tatsuta Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|35|35|N|135|41|15|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]]
| founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sangō, Nara|Sangō]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||龍田神社|Tatsuta-jinja}}. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). {{otheruses|奈良県生駒郡三郷町にある神社|奈良県生駒郡斑鳩町にある神社|龍田神社}} {{神社 |名称 = 龍田大社 |画像 = [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, haiden.jpg|270px]]
拝殿 {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |所在地 = [[奈良県]][[生駒郡]][[三郷町]]立野南1丁目29-1 |ISO = JP-29 |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 35|緯度秒 = 35.1 |経度度 =135|経度分 = 41|経度秒 = 14.4 |祭神 = [[天御柱命]]
[[国御柱命]] |社格 = [[式内社]]（[[名神大社|名神大]]2座）
[[二十二社]]（中七社）
旧[[官幣大社]]
[[別表神社]] |創建 = 伝・第10代[[崇神天皇]]年間 |本殿 = [[春日造]] |例祭 = [[4月4日]] |神事 = 滝祭（[[4月3日]]）
風鎮大祭（7月第1日曜日）
秋季大祭（10月第3土日曜日） |地図 = Japan Nara }} [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|{{center|鳥居}}]] '''龍田大社'''（たつたたいしゃ）は、[[奈良県]][[生駒郡]][[三郷町]]立野南にある[[神社]]。[[式内社]]（[[名神大社]]）、[[二十二社]]（中七社）の一社。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[官幣大社]]で、現在は[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]。旧称は「龍田神社」。[[風]]の神（[[風神]]）[http://yamatoji.nara-kankou.or.jp/01shaji/01jinja/02west_area/tatsutataisha/]として古くから信仰を集める。 == 祭神 == 祭神は次の2柱。 :* [[天御柱命]]（あめのみはしらのみこと） - 右殿。 :* [[国御柱命]]（くにのみはしらのみこと） - 左殿。 龍田の風神と総称され、[[廣瀬大社|広瀬]]の[[水神]]と並び称された。同社の[[祝詞]]などでは、天御柱命は[[シナツヒコ|級長津彦命]]（男神）、国御柱命は級長戸辺命（女神）のこととされている。 == 歴史 == === 概史 === 『[[延喜式]]』[[祝詞]]の「龍田風神祭祝詞」によれば、[[崇神天皇]]の時代、数年に渡って凶作が続き疫病が流行したため、天皇自ら天神地祇を祀って祈願したところ、夢で天御柱命・国御柱命の二柱の神を龍田山に祀れというお告げがあり、これによって創建されたという。 [[六国史|国史]]では、[[天武天皇]]4年（[[675年]]）4月10日に勅使を遣わして風神を龍田立野に祀り、大忌神を広瀬河曲に祀ったと『[[日本書紀]]』の記述が初見である。 [[延長 (元号)|延長]]5年（[[927年]]）成立の『[[延喜式]]』[[延喜式神名帳|神名帳]]では[[大和国]][[平群郡]]に「龍田坐天御柱国御柱神社二座 並名神大 月次新嘗」として、二座が[[名神大社]]に列するとともに朝廷の[[月次祭]]・[[新嘗祭]]で[[幣帛]]に預かった旨が記載されている。 [[明治維新]]後、[[1871年]]（[[明治]]4年）に[[近代社格制度]]において「龍田神社」として[[官幣大社]]に列した。 [[1948年]]（[[昭和]]23年）に[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]に加列されている。 [[2020年]]（[[令和]]2年）6月に、龍田古道と[[亀の瀬]]と共に龍田大社が[[日本遺産]]に認定された。[https://kamenose.jp/] [[紅葉]]の名所としても有名である。摂社龍田比売社の祭神である[[龍田姫]]は秋の女神とされ、古来より多くの歌に詠まれてきた。 === 神階 === * [[弘仁]]13年（[[822年]]）[[8月3日]]、従五位下 （『[[日本紀略]]』） - 表記は「竜田神」。 * [[嘉祥]]3年（[[850年]]）[[7月11日]]、従五位上 （『[[日本文徳天皇実録]]』） - 表記は「竜田天御柱命神」、「国御柱命神」。 * [[仁寿]]2年（[[852年]]）[[7月25日]]、従四位下 （『日本文徳天皇実録』） - 表記は「天御柱命神」、「国御柱命神」。 * 仁寿2年（852年）[[10月2日]]、従三位 （『日本文徳天皇実録』） - 表記は「天御柱命神」、「国御柱命神」。 * [[天安 (日本)|天安]]3年（[[859年]]）[[1月27日]]、従三位から正三位 （『[[日本三代実録]]』） - 表記は「竜田神」。 == 主な祭事 == * 滝祭（[[4月3日]]） * 風鎮大祭（7月第1日曜日） * 秋季大祭（10月第3土日曜日） == 境内 == * 本殿2棟 ** 左殿 ** 右殿 * 祝詞殿 * 神饌所 * 龍田比売命社 - 祭神：[[龍田姫|龍田比売命]] * 龍田比古命社 - 祭神：龍田比古命 * [[天照大御神]][[住吉大神]]社 * [[枚岡神社|枚岡大神]][[春日大神]]社 * [[高望王]]妃社 * 拝殿 * 白龍神社 * 龍田[[恵美須]]神社 * 三室[[稲荷神社]] * 旧神饌所 * 祈禱参集殿 * 廻廊 * 社務所 * 神楽殿（旧拝殿） * 下照神社 - 祖霊社。 * 遥拝所 * 境外社 ** 神奈備神社 * 飛び地 ** 御座峰 ** [[三室山 (三郷町)|三室山]] ** 岩瀬の杜 ファイル:龍田大社神楽殿（旧拝殿） - DHu0lzAUQAE7h f.jpg|神楽殿（旧拝殿） ファイル:龍田大社摂社（左側） - DT9hTwdU8AAoY6Y.jpg|摂社龍田比古龍田比売神社（内陣左側） ファイル:龍田大社末社（右側） - DT9hVDJU8AAmBQt.jpg|末社（内陣右側） ファイル:末社白龍神社 - DT9gOKMWsAAb8-R.jpg|白龍神社 ファイル:末社龍田恵美須神社 - DT9gQUfVQAA-8hf.jpg|龍田恵美須神社 ファイル:三室稲荷神社 - DT9gRqSU8AA-KtN.jpg|三室稲荷神社 ファイル:下照神社本殿 - DT9haI WkAAdQn.jpg|下照神社 == 文化財 == === 重要文化財 === * 宝相華彩絵奚婁鼓胴（ほうそうげさいえ けいろうこどう）（工芸品） - [[平安時代]]の作。[[1995年]]（[[平成]]7年）6月15日指定{{国指定文化財等データベース|201|7323|宝相華彩絵奚婁鼓胴}}。 === 現存しない文化財 === * [[多宝塔]] - 「大和名所圖會」[[寛政]]3年（[[1791年]]）や、「壬申検査関係写真」（[[1872年]]（[[明治]]5年））から多宝塔が確認できるが現存しない。形状は[[吉田寺 (奈良県斑鳩町)|吉田寺]]多宝塔に酷似する。 {{節スタブ}} == 現地情報 == '''所在地''' * [[奈良県]][[生駒郡]][[三郷町]]立野南1-29-1 '''交通アクセス''' * 鉄道 ** [[西日本旅客鉄道|JR西日本]][[大和路線]] [[三郷駅 (奈良県)|三郷駅]] （徒歩約8分） ** [[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[近鉄生駒線|生駒線]] [[信貴山下駅]] （徒歩約15分） == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 関連図書 == * 安津素彦・梅田義彦編集兼監修者『神道辞典』[[神社新報]]社、1968年、37頁 * [[白井永二]]・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、217頁 * [[菅田正昭]]『日本の神社を知る「事典」』[[日本文芸社]]、1989年、187-189頁 * 上山春平他『日本「神社」総覧』[[新人物往来社]]、1992年、222-223頁 == 関連項目 == * [[龍田神社]] == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Tatsuta-taisha}} * [http://www.tatsutataisha.jp/index.php 龍田大社] - 公式サイト * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=20402 龍田坐天御柱国御柱神社二座] - 國學院大學21世紀COEプログラム「神道・神社史料集成」 *[https://kamenose.jp/ 日本遺産「龍田古道・亀の瀬」推進協議会事務局]−公式サイト {{二十二社}} {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たつたたいしや}} [[Category:奈良県の神社]] [[Category:名神大社]] [[Category:大和国の式内社|名たつたたいしや]] [[Category:二十二社]] [[Category:官幣大社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:奈良県の重要文化財]] [[Category:大社]] [[Category:三郷町の建築物]] [[Category:三郷町の歴史]]

{{otheruses|奈良県生駒郡三郷町にある神社|奈良県生駒郡斑鳩町にある神社|龍田神社}} {{神社 |名称 = 龍田大社 |画像 = [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, haiden.jpg|270px]]
拝殿
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |所在地 = [[奈良県]][[生駒郡]][[三郷町]]立野南1丁目29-1 |ISO = JP-29 |緯度度 = 34|緯度分 = 35|緯度秒 = 35.1 |経度度 =135|経度分 = 41|経度秒 = 14.4 |祭神 = [[天御柱命]]
[[国御柱命]] |社格 = [[式内社]]（[[名神大社|名神大]]2座）
[[二十二社]]（中七社）
旧[[官幣大社]]
[[別表神社]] |創建 = 伝・第10代[[崇神天皇]]年間 |本殿 = [[春日造]] |例祭 = [[4月4日]] |神事 = 滝祭（[[4月3日]]）
風鎮大祭（7月第1日曜日）
秋季大祭（10月第3土日曜日） |地図 = Japan Nara }} [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|{{center|鳥居}}]] '''龍田大社'''（たつたたいしゃ）は、[[奈良県]][[生駒郡]][[三郷町]]立野南にある[[神社]]。[[式内社]]（[[名神大社]]）、[[二十二社]]（中七社）の一社。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[官幣大社]]で、現在は[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]。旧称は「龍田神社」。[[風]]の神（[[風神]]）[http://yamatoji.nara-kankou.or.jp/01shaji/01jinja/02west_area/tatsutataisha/]として古くから信仰を集める。 == 祭神 == 祭神は次の2柱。 :* [[天御柱命]]（あめのみはしらのみこと） - 右殿。 :* [[国御柱命]]（くにのみはしらのみこと） - 左殿。 龍田の風神と総称され、[[廣瀬大社|広瀬]]の[[水神]]と並び称された。同社の[[祝詞]]などでは、天御柱命は[[シナツヒコ|級長津彦命]]（男神）、国御柱命は級長戸辺命（女神）のこととされている。 == 歴史 == === 概史 === 『[[延喜式]]』[[祝詞]]の「龍田風神祭祝詞」によれば、[[崇神天皇]]の時代、数年に渡って凶作が続き疫病が流行したため、天皇自ら天神地祇を祀って祈願したところ、夢で天御柱命・国御柱命の二柱の神を龍田山に祀れというお告げがあり、これによって創建されたという。 [[六国史|国史]]では、[[天武天皇]]4年（[[675年]]）4月10日に勅使を遣わして風神を龍田立野に祀り、大忌神を広瀬河曲に祀ったと『[[日本書紀]]』の記述が初見である。 [[延長 (元号)|延長]]5年（[[927年]]）成立の『[[延喜式]]』[[延喜式神名帳|神名帳]]では[[大和国]][[平群郡]]に「龍田坐天御柱国御柱神社二座 並名神大 月次新嘗」として、二座が[[名神大社]]に列するとともに朝廷の[[月次祭]]・[[新嘗祭]]で[[幣帛]]に預かった旨が記載されている。 [[明治維新]]後、[[1871年]]（[[明治]]4年）に[[近代社格制度]]において「龍田神社」として[[官幣大社]]に列した。 [[1948年]]（[[昭和]]23年）に[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]に加列されている。 [[2020年]]（[[令和]]2年）6月に、龍田古道と[[亀の瀬]]と共に龍田大社が[[日本遺産]]に認定された。[https://kamenose.jp/] [[紅葉]]の名所としても有名である。摂社龍田比売社の祭神である[[龍田姫]]は秋の女神とされ、古来より多くの歌に詠まれてきた。 === 神階 === * [[弘仁]]13年（[[822年]]）[[8月3日]]、従五位下 （『[[日本紀略]]』） - 表記は「竜田神」。 * [[嘉祥]]3年（[[850年]]）[[7月11日]]、従五位上 （『[[日本文徳天皇実録]]』） - 表記は「竜田天御柱命神」、「国御柱命神」。 * [[仁寿]]2年（[[852年]]）[[7月25日]]、従四位下 （『日本文徳天皇実録』） - 表記は「天御柱命神」、「国御柱命神」。 * 仁寿2年（852年）[[10月2日]]、従三位 （『日本文徳天皇実録』） - 表記は「天御柱命神」、「国御柱命神」。 * [[天安 (日本)|天安]]3年（[[859年]]）[[1月27日]]、従三位から正三位 （『[[日本三代実録]]』） - 表記は「竜田神」。 == 主な祭事 == * 滝祭（[[4月3日]]） * 風鎮大祭（7月第1日曜日） * 秋季大祭（10月第3土日曜日） == 境内 == * 本殿2棟 ** 左殿 ** 右殿 * 祝詞殿 * 神饌所 * 龍田比売命社 - 祭神：[[龍田姫|龍田比売命]] * 龍田比古命社 - 祭神：龍田比古命 * [[天照大御神]][[住吉大神]]社 * [[枚岡神社|枚岡大神]][[春日大神]]社 * [[高望王]]妃社 * 拝殿 * 白龍神社 * 龍田[[恵美須]]神社 * 三室[[稲荷神社]] * 旧神饌所 * 祈禱参集殿 * 廻廊 * 社務所 * 神楽殿（旧拝殿） * 下照神社 - 祖霊社。 * 遥拝所 * 境外社 ** 神奈備神社 * 飛び地 ** 御座峰 ** [[三室山 (三郷町)|三室山]] ** 岩瀬の杜 ファイル:龍田大社神楽殿（旧拝殿） - DHu0lzAUQAE7h f.jpg|神楽殿（旧拝殿） ファイル:龍田大社摂社（左側） - DT9hTwdU8AAoY6Y.jpg|摂社龍田比古龍田比売神社（内陣左側） ファイル:龍田大社末社（右側） - DT9hVDJU8AAmBQt.jpg|末社（内陣右側） ファイル:末社白龍神社 - DT9gOKMWsAAb8-R.jpg|白龍神社 ファイル:末社龍田恵美須神社 - DT9gQUfVQAA-8hf.jpg|龍田恵美須神社 ファイル:三室稲荷神社 - DT9gRqSU8AA-KtN.jpg|三室稲荷神社 ファイル:下照神社本殿 - DT9haI WkAAdQn.jpg|下照神社 == 文化財 == === 重要文化財 === * 宝相華彩絵奚婁鼓胴（ほうそうげさいえ けいろうこどう）（工芸品） - [[平安時代]]の作。[[1995年]]（[[平成]]7年）6月15日指定{{国指定文化財等データベース|201|7323|宝相華彩絵奚婁鼓胴}}。 === 現存しない文化財 === * [[多宝塔]] - 「大和名所圖會」[[寛政]]3年（[[1791年]]）や、「壬申検査関係写真」（[[1872年]]（[[明治]]5年））から多宝塔が確認できるが現存しない。形状は[[吉田寺 (奈良県斑鳩町)|吉田寺]]多宝塔に酷似する。 {{節スタブ}} == 現地情報 == '''所在地''' * [[奈良県]][[生駒郡]][[三郷町]]立野南1-29-1 '''交通アクセス''' * 鉄道 ** [[西日本旅客鉄道|JR西日本]][[大和路線]] [[三郷駅 (奈良県)|三郷駅]] （徒歩約8分） ** [[近畿日本鉄道|近鉄]][[近鉄生駒線|生駒線]] [[信貴山下駅]] （徒歩約15分） == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 関連図書 == * 安津素彦・梅田義彦編集兼監修者『神道辞典』[[神社新報]]社、1968年、37頁 * [[白井永二]]・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、217頁 * [[菅田正昭]]『日本の神社を知る「事典」』[[日本文芸社]]、1989年、187-189頁 * 上山春平他『日本「神社」総覧』[[新人物往来社]]、1992年、222-223頁 == 関連項目 == * [[龍田神社]] == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Tatsuta-taisha}} * [http://www.tatsutataisha.jp/index.php 龍田大社] - 公式サイト * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=20402 龍田坐天御柱国御柱神社二座] - 國學院大學21世紀COEプログラム「神道・神社史料集成」 *[https://kamenose.jp/ 日本遺産「龍田古道・亀の瀬」推進協議会事務局]−公式サイト {{二十二社}} {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たつたたいしや}} [[Category:奈良県の神社]] [[Category:名神大社]] [[Category:大和国の式内社|名たつたたいしや]] [[Category:二十二社]] [[Category:官幣大社]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:奈良県の重要文化財]] [[Category:大社]] [[Category:三郷町の建築物]] [[Category:三郷町の歴史]]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] to [[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]][[en:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} | image = Tatsuta-Taisha03.JPG | caption = [[Torii]] at the entrance of Tatsuta Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|35|35|N|135|41|15|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]]
| founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = [[Kasuga-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sangō, Nara|Sangō]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||龍田神社|Tatsuta-jinja}}. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tatsuta Shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Kasuga-zukuri]] {{Shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} | image = Tatsuta-Taisha03.JPG | caption = [[Torii]] at the entrance of Tatsuta Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|35|35|N|135|41|15|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]]
| founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = [[Kasuga-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sangō, Nara|Sangō]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||龍田神社|Tatsuta-jinja}}. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tatsuta Shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Kasuga-zukuri]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:pages from wikipedia]] {{Shinto-stub}}

The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||龍田神社|Tatsuta-jinja}}. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} | image = Tatsuta-Taisha03.JPG | caption = [[Torii]] at the entrance of Tatsuta Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|35|35|N|135|41|15|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]]
| founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = [[Kasuga-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sangō, Nara|Sangō]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||龍田神社|Tatsuta-jinja}}. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tatsuta Shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Kasuga-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia04]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia04]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia04]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia04]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia31]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia31]] [[Category:dd]] {{Shinto-stub}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} | image = Tatsuta-Taisha03.JPG | caption = [[Torii]] at the entrance of Tatsuta Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|35|35|N|135|41|15|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]]
| founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = [[Kasuga-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sangō, Nara|Sangō]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||龍田神社|Tatsuta-jinja}}. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = [[Kasuga-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sangō, Nara|Sangō]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||龍田神社|Tatsuta-jinja}}. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tatsuta Shrine}} [[category:kk]][[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:kk]][[category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[category:kk]][[category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[category:kk]][[category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[category:kk]][[category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[category:kk]][[category:Taisha]] [[category:kk]][[category:Kasuga-zukuri]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:dd]] {{Shinto-stub}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} | image = Tatsuta-Taisha03.JPG | caption = [[Torii]] at the entrance of Tatsuta Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|35|35|N|135|41|15|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]]
| founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = [[Kasuga-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sangō, Nara|Sangō]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||龍田神社|Tatsuta-jinja}}. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||龍田神社|Tatsuta-jinja}}. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and one of the {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社}} (Middle Seven Shrines). Under the former shrine ranking system, it was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. It was formerly known as "Tatsuta Shrine." The shrine has long been venerated as the home of the deity of {{ill|Wind|ja|風}} ({{ill|Fūjin|ja|風神}}).[http://yamatoji.nara-kankou.or.jp/01shaji/01jinja/02west_area/tatsutataisha/] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha of Yamato Province]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sangō, Nara]] [[Category:History of Sangō, Nara]] [[ja:龍田大社]] == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Overview== == Tatsuta Taisha == {{otheruses|Shrine in Sangō, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|Shrine in Ikaruga, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|{{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社}}}} {{Shrine |name = Tatsuta Taisha |image = [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, haiden.jpg|270px]]

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Ikoma District|ja|生駒郡}}{{ill|Sangō, Nara|ja|三郷町}} Tateno Minami 1-chome 29-1 |ISO = JP-29 |lat_deg = 34|lat_min = 35|lat_sec = 35.1 |lon_deg = 135|lon_min = 41|lon_sec = 14.4 |enshrined_deities = {{ill|Ame no Mihashira no Mikoto|ja|天御柱命}}

{{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社}} (Middle Seven Shrines)
Former {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社}}
{{ill|Beppyo Shrines|ja|別表神社}} |established = Traditionally during the reign of the 10th {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} |main_hall = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造}} |annual_festival = {{ill|April 4|ja|4月4日}} |rituals = Taki Festival ({{ill|April 3|ja|4月3日}})
Fūchin Grand Festival (1st Sunday of July)
Autumn Grand Festival (3rd Saturday and Sunday of October) |map = Japan Nara }} [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|{{center|{{ill|Torii|ja|鳥居}}}}]] '''Tatsuta Taisha''' (龍田大社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Tateno Minami, {{ill|Sangō, Nara|ja|三郷町}}, {{ill|Ikoma District|ja|生駒郡}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and one of the {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社}} (Middle Seven Shrines). Under the former shrine ranking system, it was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. It was formerly known as "Tatsuta Shrine." The shrine has long been venerated as the home of the deity of {{ill|Wind|ja|風}} ({{ill|Fūjin|ja|風神}}).[http://yamatoji.nara-kankou.or.jp/01shaji/01jinja/02west_area/tatsutataisha/] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha of Yamato Province]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sangō, Nara]] [[Category:History of Sangō, Nara]] [[ja:龍田大社]] == History == === Overview === According to the "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}" {{ill|Norito|ja|祝詞}} in the "Tatsuta Wind God Festival Norito," during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, years of poor harvests and widespread epidemics prompted the emperor to personally pray to the heavenly and earthly deities. In a dream, he received a divine message instructing him to enshrine the two deities, {{ill|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|天御柱命}} and {{ill|Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|国御柱命}}, on Mount Tatsuta, which led to the founding of the shrine. In the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}, the earliest record appears in the "{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}," stating that on April 10, the 4th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (675), an imperial envoy was dispatched to enshrine the Wind God at Tatsuta-Tateno and the Great Taboo God at Hirose Kawamuki. The "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}" {{ill|Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|Encho era|ja|延長}} (927), lists "Tatsuta-no-Iwato-no-Ame-no-Mihashira-Kuni-no-Mihashira Jinja" with two deities in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}'s {{ill|Heguri District|ja|平群郡}}. They are classified as {{ill|Myojin Taisha|ja|名神大社}} and received offerings from the imperial court during the {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社}} in 1871 (4th year of Meiji) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}}. In 1948 (23rd year of Showa), it was included as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. In June 2020 (2nd year of Reiwa), Tatsuta Taisha, along with the {{ill|Tatsuta Ancient Road|ja|龍田古道}} and {{ill|Kame-no-se|ja|亀の瀬}}, was designated as a {{ill|Japan Heritage Site|ja|日本遺産}}. [https://kamenose.jp/] The shrine is also famous as a scenic spot for {{ill|autumn foliage|ja|紅葉}}. The deity enshrined in the auxiliary shrine, Tatsuta Hime-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Tatsuta-hime|ja|龍田姫}}), is revered as a goddess of autumn and has been celebrated in many poems since ancient times. === Ranks of Deities === * 13th year of {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (822), August 3: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." * 3rd year of {{ill|Kashō era|ja|嘉祥}} (850), July 11: Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of {{ill|Ninju era|ja|仁寿}} (852), July 25: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of Ninju (852), October 2: Junior Third Rank (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 3rd year of {{ill|Ten'an era|ja|天安}} (859), January 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank to Senior Third Rank (from "{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} | image = Tatsuta-Taisha03.JPG | caption = [[Torii]] at the entrance of Tatsuta Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|35|35|N|135|41|15|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]]
| founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = [[Kasuga-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sangō, Nara|Sangō]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||龍田神社|Tatsuta-jinja}}. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Overview== == Tatsuta Taisha == {{otheruses|Shrine in Sangō, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|Shrine in Ikaruga, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|{{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社}}}} {{Shrine |name = Tatsuta Taisha |image = [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Ikoma District|ja|生駒郡}}{{ill|Sangō, Nara|ja|三郷町}} Tateno Minami 1-chome 29-1 |ISO = JP-29 |lat_deg = 34|lat_min = 35|lat_sec = 35.1 |lon_deg = 135|lon_min = 41|lon_sec = 14.4 |deity = {{ill|Ame no Mihashira no Mikoto|ja|天御柱命}}
{{ill|Kuni no Mihashira no Mikoto|ja|国御柱命}} |rank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}} 2 seats)
{{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社}} (Middle Seven Shrines)
Former {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社}}
{{ill|Beppyo Shrines|ja|別表神社}} |established = Traditionally during the reign of the 10th {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} |main_hall = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造}} |annual_festival = {{ill|April 4|ja|4月4日}} |rituals = Taki Festival ({{ill|April 3|ja|4月3日}})
Fūchin Grand Festival (1st Sunday of July)
Autumn Grand Festival (3rd Saturday and Sunday of October) |map = Japan Nara }} [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|{{center|{{ill|Torii|ja|鳥居}}}}]] '''Tatsuta Taisha''' (龍田大社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Tateno Minami, {{ill|Sangō, Nara|ja|三郷町}}, {{ill|Ikoma District|ja|生駒郡}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and one of the {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社}} (Middle Seven Shrines). Under the former shrine ranking system, it was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. It was formerly known as "Tatsuta Shrine." The shrine has long been venerated as the home of the deity of {{ill|Wind|ja|風}} ({{ill|Fūjin|ja|風神}}).[http://yamatoji.nara-kankou.or.jp/01shaji/01jinja/02west_area/tatsutataisha/] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha of Yamato Province]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Beppyo Shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sangō, Nara]] [[Category:History of Sangō, Nara]] [[ja:龍田大社]] == History == === Overview === According to the "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}" {{ill|Norito|ja|祝詞}} in the "Tatsuta Wind God Festival Norito," during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, years of poor harvests and widespread epidemics prompted the emperor to personally pray to the heavenly and earthly deities. In a dream, he received a divine message instructing him to enshrine the two deities, {{ill|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|天御柱命}} and {{ill|Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|国御柱命}}, on Mount Tatsuta, which led to the founding of the shrine. In the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}, the earliest record appears in the "{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}," stating that on April 10, the 4th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (675), an imperial envoy was dispatched to enshrine the Wind God at Tatsuta-Tateno and the Great Taboo God at Hirose Kawamuki. The "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}" {{ill|Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|Encho era|ja|延長}} (927), lists "Tatsuta-no-Iwato-no-Ame-no-Mihashira-Kuni-no-Mihashira Jinja" with two deities in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}'s {{ill|Heguri District|ja|平群郡}}. They are classified as {{ill|Myojin Taisha|ja|名神大社}} and received offerings from the imperial court during the {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社}} in 1871 (4th year of Meiji) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}}. In 1948 (23rd year of Showa), it was included as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. In June 2020 (2nd year of Reiwa), Tatsuta Taisha, along with the {{ill|Tatsuta Ancient Road|ja|龍田古道}} and {{ill|Kame-no-se|ja|亀の瀬}}, was designated as a {{ill|Japan Heritage Site|ja|日本遺産}}. [https://kamenose.jp/] The shrine is also famous as a scenic spot for {{ill|autumn foliage|ja|紅葉}}. The deity enshrined in the auxiliary shrine, Tatsuta Hime-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Tatsuta-hime|ja|龍田姫}}), is revered as a goddess of autumn and has been celebrated in many poems since ancient times. === Ranks of Deities === * 13th year of {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (822), August 3: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." * 3rd year of {{ill|Kashō era|ja|嘉祥}} (850), July 11: Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of {{ill|Ninju era|ja|仁寿}} (852), July 25: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of Ninju (852), October 2: Junior Third Rank (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 3rd year of {{ill|Ten'an era|ja|天安}} (859), January 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank to Senior Third Rank (from "{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Overview== == Tatsuta Taisha == {{otheruses|Shrine in Sangō, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|Shrine in Ikaruga, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|{{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社}}}} {{Shrine |name = Tatsuta Taisha |image = [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Ikoma District|ja|生駒郡}}{{ill|Sangō, Nara|ja|三郷町}} Tateno Minami 1-chome 29-1 |ISO = JP-29 |lat_deg = 34|lat_min = 35|lat_sec = 35.1 |lon_deg = 135|lon_min = 41|lon_sec = 14.4 |deity = {{ill|Ame no Mihashira no Mikoto|ja|天御柱命}}
{{ill|Kuni no Mihashira no Mikoto|ja|国御柱命}} |rank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}} 2 seats)
{{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社}} (Middle Seven Shrines)
Former {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社}}
{{ill|Beppyo Shrines|ja|別表神社}} |established = Traditionally during the reign of the 10th {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} |main_hall = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造}} |annual_festival = {{ill|April 4|ja|4月4日}} |rituals = Taki Festival ({{ill|April 3|ja|4月3日}})
Fūchin Grand Festival (1st Sunday of July)
Autumn Grand Festival (3rd Saturday and Sunday of October) |map = Japan Nara }} [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|{{center|{{ill|Torii|ja|鳥居}}}}]] '''Tatsuta Taisha''' (龍田大社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Tateno Minami, {{ill|Sangō, Nara|ja|三郷町}}, {{ill|Ikoma District|ja|生駒郡}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and one of the {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社}} (Middle Seven Shrines). Under the former shrine ranking system, it was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. It was formerly known as "Tatsuta Shrine." The shrine has long been venerated as the home of the deity of {{ill|Wind|ja|風}} ({{ill|Fūjin|ja|風神}}).[http://yamatoji.nara-kankou.or.jp/01shaji/01jinja/02west_area/tatsutataisha/] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha of Yamato Province]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sangō, Nara]] [[Category:History of Sangō, Nara]] [[ja:龍田大社]] == History == === Overview === According to the "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}" {{ill|Norito|ja|祝詞}} in the "Tatsuta Wind God Festival Norito," during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, years of poor harvests and widespread epidemics prompted the emperor to personally pray to the heavenly and earthly deities. In a dream, he received a divine message instructing him to enshrine the two deities, {{ill|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|天御柱命}} and {{ill|Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|国御柱命}}, on Mount Tatsuta, which led to the founding of the shrine. In the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}, the earliest record appears in the "{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}," stating that on April 10, the 4th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (675), an imperial envoy was dispatched to enshrine the Wind God at Tatsuta-Tateno and the Great Taboo God at Hirose Kawamuki. The "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}" {{ill|Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|Encho era|ja|延長}} (927), lists "Tatsuta-no-Iwato-no-Ame-no-Mihashira-Kuni-no-Mihashira Jinja" with two deities in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}'s {{ill|Heguri District|ja|平群郡}}. They are classified as {{ill|Myojin Taisha|ja|名神大社}} and received offerings from the imperial court during the {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社}} in 1871 (4th year of Meiji) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}}. In 1948 (23rd year of Showa), it was included as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. In June 2020 (2nd year of Reiwa), Tatsuta Taisha, along with the {{ill|Tatsuta Ancient Road|ja|龍田古道}} and {{ill|Kame-no-se|ja|亀の瀬}}, was designated as a {{ill|Japan Heritage Site|ja|日本遺産}}. [https://kamenose.jp/] The shrine is also famous as a scenic spot for {{ill|autumn foliage|ja|紅葉}}. The deity enshrined in the auxiliary shrine, Tatsuta Hime-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Tatsuta-hime|ja|龍田姫}}), is revered as a goddess of autumn and has been celebrated in many poems since ancient times. === Ranks of Deities === * 13th year of {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (822), August 3: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." * 3rd year of {{ill|Kashō era|ja|嘉祥}} (850), July 11: Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of {{ill|Ninju era|ja|仁寿}} (852), July 25: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of Ninju (852), October 2: Junior Third Rank (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 3rd year of {{ill|Ten'an era|ja|天安}} (859), January 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank to Senior Third Rank (from "{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tatsuta Shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Kasuga-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]{{Shinto-stub}}

Autumn Grand Festival (3rd Saturday and Sunday of October) |map = Japan Nara }} [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|{{center|{{ill|Torii|ja|鳥居}}}}]] '''Tatsuta Taisha''' (龍田大社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Tateno Minami, {{ill|Sangō, Nara|ja|三郷町}}, {{ill|Ikoma District|ja|生駒郡}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and one of the {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社}} (Middle Seven Shrines). Under the former shrine ranking system, it was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. It was formerly known as "Tatsuta Shrine." The shrine has long been venerated as the home of the deity of {{ill|Wind|ja|風}} ({{ill|Fūjin|ja|風神}}).[http://yamatoji.nara-kankou.or.jp/01shaji/01jinja/02west_area/tatsutataisha/] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha of Yamato Province]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sangō, Nara]] [[Category:History of Sangō, Nara]] [[ja:龍田大社]] == History == === Overview === According to the "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}" {{ill|Norito|ja|祝詞}} in the "Tatsuta Wind God Festival Norito," during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, years of poor harvests and widespread epidemics prompted the emperor to personally pray to the heavenly and earthly deities. In a dream, he received a divine message instructing him to enshrine the two deities, {{ill|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|天御柱命}} and {{ill|Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|国御柱命}}, on Mount Tatsuta, which led to the founding of the shrine. In the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}, the earliest record appears in the "{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}," stating that on April 10, the 4th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (675), an imperial envoy was dispatched to enshrine the Wind God at Tatsuta-Tateno and the Great Taboo God at Hirose Kawamuki. The "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}" {{ill|Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|Encho era|ja|延長}} (927), lists "Tatsuta-no-Iwato-no-Ame-no-Mihashira-Kuni-no-Mihashira Jinja" with two deities in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}'s {{ill|Heguri District|ja|平群郡}}. They are classified as {{ill|Myojin Taisha|ja|名神大社}} and received offerings from the imperial court during the {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社}} in 1871 (4th year of Meiji) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}}. In 1948 (23rd year of Showa), it was included as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. In June 2020 (2nd year of Reiwa), Tatsuta Taisha, along with the {{ill|Tatsuta Ancient Road|ja|龍田古道}} and {{ill|Kame-no-se|ja|亀の瀬}}, was designated as a {{ill|Japan Heritage Site|ja|日本遺産}}. [https://kamenose.jp/] The shrine is also famous as a scenic spot for {{ill|autumn foliage|ja|紅葉}}. The deity enshrined in the auxiliary shrine, Tatsuta Hime-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Tatsuta-hime|ja|龍田姫}}), is revered as a goddess of autumn and has been celebrated in many poems since ancient times. === Ranks of Deities === * 13th year of {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (822), August 3: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." * 3rd year of {{ill|Kashō era|ja|嘉祥}} (850), July 11: Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of {{ill|Ninju era|ja|仁寿}} (852), July 25: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of Ninju (852), October 2: Junior Third Rank (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 3rd year of {{ill|Ten'an era|ja|天安}} (859), January 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank to Senior Third Rank (from "{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tatsuta Shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Kasuga-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]{{Shinto-stub}}

| founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = [[Kasuga-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sangō, Nara|Sangō]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||龍田神社|Tatsuta-jinja}}. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Overview== == Tatsuta Taisha == {{otheruses|Shrine in Sangō, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|Shrine in Ikaruga, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|{{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社}}}} {{Shrine |name = Tatsuta Taisha |image = [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, haiden.jpg|270px]]

[https://kamenose.jp/] The shrine is also famous as a scenic spot for {{ill|autumn foliage|ja|紅葉}}. In a dream, he received a divine message instructing him to enshrine the two deities, {{ill|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|天御柱命}} and {{ill|Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|国御柱命}}, on Mount Tatsuta, which led to the founding of the shrine. In the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}, the earliest record appears in the "{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}," stating that on April 10, the 4th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (675), an imperial envoy was dispatched to enshrine the Wind God at Tatsuta-Tateno and the Great Taboo God at Hirose Kawamuki. The "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}" {{ill|Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|Encho era|ja|延長}} (927), lists "Tatsuta-no-Iwato-no-Ame-no-Mihashira-Kuni-no-Mihashira Jinja" with two deities in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}'s {{ill|Heguri District|ja|平群郡}}. They are classified as {{ill|Myojin Taisha|ja|名神大社}} and received offerings from the imperial court during the {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社}} in 1871 (4th year of Meiji) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}}. In 1948 (23rd year of Showa), it was included as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. In June 2020 (2nd year of Reiwa), Tatsuta Taisha, along with the {{ill|Tatsuta Ancient Road|ja|龍田古道}} and {{ill|Kame-no-se|ja|亀の瀬}}, was designated as a {{ill|Japan Heritage Site|ja|日本遺産}}. [https://kamenose.jp/] The shrine is also famous as a scenic spot for {{ill|autumn foliage|ja|紅葉}}. The deity enshrined in the auxiliary shrine, Tatsuta Hime-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Tatsuta-hime|ja|龍田姫}}), is revered as a goddess of autumn and has been celebrated in many poems since ancient times. === Ranks of Deities === * 13th year of {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (822), August 3: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." * 3rd year of {{ill|Kashō era|ja|嘉祥}} (850), July 11: Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of {{ill|Ninju era|ja|仁寿}} (852), July 25: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of Ninju (852), October 2: Junior Third Rank (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 3rd year of {{ill|Ten'an era|ja|天安}} (859), January 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank to Senior Third Rank (from "{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = [[Kasuga-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sangō, Nara|Sangō]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||龍田神社|Tatsuta-jinja}}. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Overview== == Tatsuta Taisha == {{otheruses|Shrine in Sangō, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|Shrine in Ikaruga, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|{{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社}}}} {{Shrine |name = Tatsuta Taisha |image = [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, haiden.jpg|270px]]

It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and one of the {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社}} (Middle Seven Shrines). Under the former shrine ranking system, it was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. It was formerly known as "Tatsuta Shrine." The shrine has long been venerated as the home of the deity of {{ill|Wind|ja|風}} ({{ill|Fūjin|ja|風神}}).[http://yamatoji.nara-kankou.or.jp/01shaji/01jinja/02west_area/tatsutataisha/] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha of Yamato Province]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sangō, Nara]] [[Category:History of Sangō, Nara]] [[ja:龍田大社]] == History == === Overview === According to the "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}" {{ill|Norito|ja|祝詞}} in the "Tatsuta Wind God Festival Norito," during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, years of poor harvests and widespread epidemics prompted the emperor to personally pray to the heavenly and earthly deities. In a dream, he received a divine message instructing him to enshrine the two deities, {{ill|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|天御柱命}} and {{ill|Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|国御柱命}}, on Mount Tatsuta, which led to the founding of the shrine. In the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}, the earliest record appears in the "{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}," stating that on April 10, the 4th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (675), an imperial envoy was dispatched to enshrine the Wind God at Tatsuta-Tateno and the Great Taboo God at Hirose Kawamuki. The "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}" {{ill|Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|Encho era|ja|延長}} (927), lists "Tatsuta-no-Iwato-no-Ame-no-Mihashira-Kuni-no-Mihashira Jinja" with two deities in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}'s {{ill|Heguri District|ja|平群郡}}. They are classified as {{ill|Myojin Taisha|ja|名神大社}} and received offerings from the imperial court during the {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社}} in 1871 (4th year of Meiji) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度}}. In 1948 (23rd year of Showa), it was included as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. In June 2020 (2nd year of Reiwa), Tatsuta Taisha, along with the {{ill|Tatsuta Ancient Road|ja|龍田古道}} and {{ill|Kame-no-se|ja|亀の瀬}}, was designated as a {{ill|Japan Heritage Site|ja|日本遺産}}. [https://kamenose.jp/] The shrine is also famous as a scenic spot for {{ill|autumn foliage|ja|紅葉}}. The deity enshrined in the auxiliary shrine, Tatsuta Hime-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Tatsuta-hime|ja|龍田姫}}), is revered as a goddess of autumn and has been celebrated in many poems since ancient times. === Ranks of Deities === * 13th year of {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (822), August 3: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." * 3rd year of {{ill|Kashō era|ja|嘉祥}} (850), July 11: Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of {{ill|Ninju era|ja|仁寿}} (852), July 25: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of Ninju (852), October 2: Junior Third Rank (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 3rd year of {{ill|Ten'an era|ja|天安}} (859), January 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank to Senior Third Rank (from "{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. In June 2020 (2nd year of Reiwa), Tatsuta Taisha, along with the {{ill|Tatsuta Ancient Road|ja|龍田古道}} and {{ill|Kame-no-se|ja|亀の瀬}}, was designated as a {{ill|Japan Heritage Site|ja|日本遺産}}. [https://kamenose.jp/] The shrine is also famous as a scenic spot for {{ill|autumn foliage|ja|紅葉}}. The deity enshrined in the auxiliary shrine, Tatsuta Hime-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Tatsuta-hime|ja|龍田姫}}), is revered as a goddess of autumn and has been celebrated in many poems since ancient times. === Ranks of Deities === * 13th year of {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (822), August 3: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." * 3rd year of {{ill|Kashō era|ja|嘉祥}} (850), July 11: Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of {{ill|Ninju era|ja|仁寿}} (852), July 25: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of Ninju (852), October 2: Junior Third Rank (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 3rd year of {{ill|Ten'an era|ja|天安}} (859), January 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank to Senior Third Rank (from "{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} | image = Tatsuta-Taisha03.JPG | caption = [[Torii]] at the entrance of Tatsuta Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|35|35|N|135|41|15|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]]
| founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = [[Kasuga-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sangō, Nara|Sangō]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||龍田神社|Tatsuta-jinja}}. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Overview== == Tatsuta Taisha == {{otheruses|Shrine in Sangō, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|Shrine in Ikaruga, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|{{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社}}}} {{Shrine |name = Tatsuta Taisha |image = [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}{{ill|Ikoma District|ja|生駒郡}}{{ill|Sangō, Nara|ja|三郷町}} Tateno Minami 1-chome 29-1 |ISO = JP-29 |lat_deg = 34|lat_min = 35|lat_sec = 35.1 |lon_deg = 135|lon_min = 41|lon_sec = 14.4 |deity = {{ill|Ame no Mihashira no Mikoto|ja|天御柱命}}
{{ill|Kuni no Mihashira no Mikoto|ja|国御柱命}} |rank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}} 2 seats)
{{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社}} (Middle Seven Shrines)
Former {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社}}
{{ill|Beppyo Shrines|ja|別表神社}} |established = Traditionally during the reign of the 10th {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} |main_hall = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造}} |annual_festival = {{ill|April 4|ja|4月4日}} |rituals = Taki Festival ({{ill|April 3|ja|4月3日}})
Fūchin Grand Festival (1st Sunday of July)
Autumn Grand Festival (3rd Saturday and Sunday of October) |map = Japan Nara }} [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|{{center|{{ill|Torii|ja|鳥居}}}}]] '''Tatsuta Taisha''' (龍田大社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Tateno Minami, {{ill|Sangō, Nara|ja|三郷町}}, {{ill|Ikoma District|ja|生駒郡}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社}}) and one of the {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社}} (Middle Seven Shrines). Under the former shrine ranking system, it was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. It was formerly known as "Tatsuta Shrine." The shrine has long been venerated as the home of the deity of {{ill|Wind|ja|風}} ({{ill|Fūjin|ja|風神}}).[http://yamatoji.nara-kankou.or.jp/01shaji/01jinja/02west_area/tatsutataisha/] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha of Yamato Province]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sangō, Nara]] [[Category:History of Sangō, Nara]] [[ja:龍田大社]] == History == === Overview === According to the "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}" {{ill|Norito|ja|祝詞}} in the "Tatsuta Wind God Festival Norito," during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}}, years of poor harvests and widespread epidemics prompted the emperor to personally pray to the heavenly and earthly deities. In a dream, he received a divine message instructing him to enshrine the two deities, {{ill|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|天御柱命}} and {{ill|Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|国御柱命}}, on Mount Tatsuta, which led to the founding of the shrine. In the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史}}, the earliest record appears in the "{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}," stating that on April 10, the 4th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} (675), an imperial envoy was dispatched to enshrine the Wind God at Tatsuta-Tateno and the Great Taboo God at Hirose Kawamuki. The "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}" {{ill|Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|Encho era|ja|延長}} (927), lists "Tatsuta-no-Iwato-no-Ame-no-Mihashira-Kuni-no-Mihashira Jinja" with two deities in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}}'s {{ill|Heguri District|ja|平群郡}}. They are classified as {{ill|Myojin Taisha|ja|名神大社}} and received offerings from the imperial court during the {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭}} and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭}}. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社}} in 1871 (4th year of Meiji) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代type制度}}. In 1948 (23rd year of Showa), it was included as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社}} under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. In June 2020 (2nd year of Reiwa), Tatsuta Taisha, along with the {{ill|Tatsuta Ancient Road|ja|龍田古道}} and {{ill|Kame-no-se|ja|亀の瀬}}, was designated as a {{ill|Japan Heritage Site|ja|日本遺産}}. [https://kamenose.jp/] The shrine is also famous as a scenic spot for {{ill|autumn foliage|ja|紅葉}}. The deity enshrined in the auxiliary shrine, Tatsuta Hime-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Tatsuta-hime|ja|龍田姫}}), is revered as a goddess of autumn and has been celebrated in many poems since ancient times. === Ranks of Deities === * 13th year of {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁}} (822), August 3: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." * 3rd year of {{ill|Kashō era|ja|嘉祥}} (850), July 11: Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of {{ill|Ninju era|ja|仁寿}} (852), July 25: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of Ninju (852), October 2: Junior Third Rank (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 3rd year of {{ill|Ten'an era|ja|天安}} (859), January 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank to Senior Third Rank (from "{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{Shinto shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tatsuta Shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Kasuga-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]][[en:Tatsuta Taisha]]{{Shinto-stub}}
