It was formerly known as "Tatsuta Shrine." The shrine has long been venerated as the home of the deity of {{ill|Wind|ja|風|12=draft}} ({{ill|Fūjin|ja|風神|12=draft}}).[http://yamatoji.nara-kankou.or.jp/01shaji/01jinja/02west_area/tatsutataisha/] == History == == Overview == According to the "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}" {{ill|Norito|ja|祝詞|12=draft}} in the "Tatsuta Wind God Festival Norito," during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|12=draft}}, years of poor harvests and widespread epidemics prompted the emperor to personally pray to the heavenly and earthly deities. In a dream, he received a divine message instructing him to enshrine the two deities, {{ill|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|天御柱命|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|国御柱命|12=draft}}, on Mount Tatsuta, which led to the founding of the shrine. In the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|12=draft}}, the earliest record appears in the "{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}," stating that on April 10, the 4th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}} (675), an imperial envoy was dispatched to enshrine the Wind God at Tatsuta-Tateno and the Great Taboo God at Hirose Kawamuki. The "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}" {{ill|Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|Encho era|ja|延長|12=draft}} (927), lists "Tatsuta-no-Iwato-no-Ame-no-Mihashira-Kuni-no-Mihashira Jinja" with two deities in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Heguri District|ja|平群郡|12=draft}}. They are classified as {{ill|Myojin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}} and received offerings from the imperial court during the {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=draft}} and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭|12=draft}}. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft}} in 1871 (4th year of Meiji) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft}}. In 1948 (23rd year of Showa), it was included as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}}. In June 2020 (2nd year of Reiwa), Tatsuta Taisha, along with the {{ill|Tatsuta Ancient Road|ja|龍田古道|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kame-no-se|ja|亀の瀬|12=draft}}, was designated as a {{ill|Japan Heritage Site|ja|日本遺産|12=draft}}. [https://kamenose.jp/] The shrine is also famous as a scenic spot for {{ill|autumn foliage|ja|紅葉|12=draft}}. The deity enshrined in the auxiliary shrine, Tatsuta Hime-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Tatsuta-hime|ja|龍田姫|12=draft}}), is revered as a goddess of autumn and has been celebrated in many poems since ancient times. === Ranks of Deities === * 13th year of {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|12=draft}} (822), August 3: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." * 3rd year of {{ill|Kashō era|ja|嘉祥|12=draft}} (850), July 11: Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of {{ill|Ninju era|ja|仁寿|12=draft}} (852), July 25: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of Ninju (852), October 2: Junior Third Rank (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 3rd year of {{ill|Ten'an era|ja|天安|12=draft}} (859), January 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank to Senior Third Rank (from "{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} | image = Tatsuta-Taisha03.JPG | caption = [[Torii]] at the entrance of Tatsuta Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|35|35|N|135|41|15|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]]
| founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = [[Kasuga-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sangō, Nara|Sangō]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||龍田神社|Tatsuta-jinja}}. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Overview== == Tatsuta Taisha == {{otheruses|Shrine in Sangō, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|Shrine in Ikaruga, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|{{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社|12=draft}}}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Tatsuta Taisha |image = [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, haiden.jpg|270px]]

In 1948 (23rd year of Showa), it was included as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}}. In June 2020 (2nd year of Reiwa), Tatsuta Taisha, along with the {{ill|Tatsuta Ancient Road|ja|龍田古道|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kame-no-se|ja|亀の瀬|12=draft}}, was designated as a {{ill|Japan Heritage Site|ja|日本遺産|12=draft}}. [https://kamenose.jp/] The shrine is also famous as a scenic spot for {{ill|autumn foliage|ja|紅葉|12=draft}}. The deity enshrined in the auxiliary shrine, Tatsuta Hime-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Tatsuta-hime|ja|龍田姫|12=draft}}), is revered as a goddess of autumn and has been celebrated in many poems since ancient times. === Ranks of Deities === * 13th year of {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|12=draft}} (822), August 3: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." * 3rd year of {{ill|Kashō era|ja|嘉祥|12=draft}} (850), July 11: Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of {{ill|Ninju era|ja|仁寿|12=draft}} (852), July 25: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of Ninju (852), October 2: Junior Third Rank (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 3rd year of {{ill|Ten'an era|ja|天安|12=draft}} (859), January 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank to Senior Third Rank (from "{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} | image = Tatsuta-Taisha03.JPG | caption = [[Torii]] at the entrance of Tatsuta Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|35|35|N|135|41|15|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]]
| founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = [[Kasuga-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sangō, Nara|Sangō]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||龍田神社|Tatsuta-jinja}}. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Overview== == Tatsuta Taisha == {{otheruses|Shrine in Sangō, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|Shrine in Ikaruga, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|{{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社|12=draft}}}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Tatsuta Taisha |image = [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, haiden.jpg|270px]]

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}{{ill|Ikoma District|ja|生駒郡|12=draft}}{{ill|Sangō, Nara|ja|三郷町|12=draft}} Tateno Minami 1-chome 29-1 |ISO = JP-29 |lat_deg = 34|lat_min = 35|lat_sec = 35.1 |lon_deg = 135|lon_min = 41|lon_sec = 14.4 |deity = {{ill|Ame no Mihashira no Mikoto|ja|天御柱命|12=draft}}
{{ill|Kuni no Mihashira no Mikoto|ja|国御柱命|12=draft}} |rank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}} 2 seats)

{{ill|Beppyo Shrines|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} |established = Traditionally during the reign of the 10th {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|12=draft}} |main_hall = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造|12=draft}} |annual_festival = {{ill|April 4|ja|4月4日|12=draft}} |rituals = Taki Festival ({{ill|April 3|ja|4月3日|12=draft}})
Fūchin Grand Festival (1st Sunday of July)
Autumn Grand Festival (3rd Saturday and Sunday of October) |map = Japan Nara }} [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|{{center|{{ill|Torii|ja|鳥居|12=draft}}}}]] '''Tatsuta Taisha''' (龍田大社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in Tateno Minami, {{ill|Sangō, Nara|ja|三郷町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ikoma District|ja|生駒郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}}) and one of the {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社|12=draft}} (Middle Seven Shrines). Under the former shrine ranking system, it was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}}. It was formerly known as "Tatsuta Shrine." The shrine has long been venerated as the home of the deity of {{ill|Wind|ja|風|12=draft}} ({{ill|Fūjin|ja|風神|12=draft}}).[http://yamatoji.nara-kankou.or.jp/01shaji/01jinja/02west_area/tatsutataisha/] == History == == Overview == According to the "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}" {{ill|Norito|ja|祝詞|12=draft}} in the "Tatsuta Wind God Festival Norito," during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|12=draft}}, years of poor harvests and widespread epidemics prompted the emperor to personally pray to the heavenly and earthly deities. In a dream, he received a divine message instructing him to enshrine the two deities, {{ill|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|天御柱命|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|国御柱命|12=draft}}, on Mount Tatsuta, which led to the founding of the shrine. In the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|12=draft}}, the earliest record appears in the "{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}," stating that on April 10, the 4th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}} (675), an imperial envoy was dispatched to enshrine the Wind God at Tatsuta-Tateno and the Great Taboo God at Hirose Kawamuki. The "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}" {{ill|Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|Encho era|ja|延長|12=draft}} (927), lists "Tatsuta-no-Iwato-no-Ame-no-Mihashira-Kuni-no-Mihashira Jinja" with two deities in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Heguri District|ja|平群郡|12=draft}}. They are classified as {{ill|Myojin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}} and received offerings from the imperial court during the {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=draft}} and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭|12=draft}}. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft}} in 1871 (4th year of Meiji) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft}}. In 1948 (23rd year of Showa), it was included as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}}. In June 2020 (2nd year of Reiwa), Tatsuta Taisha, along with the {{ill|Tatsuta Ancient Road|ja|龍田古道|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kame-no-se|ja|亀の瀬|12=draft}}, was designated as a {{ill|Japan Heritage Site|ja|日本遺産|12=draft}}. [https://kamenose.jp/] The shrine is also famous as a scenic spot for {{ill|autumn foliage|ja|紅葉|12=draft}}. The deity enshrined in the auxiliary shrine, Tatsuta Hime-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Tatsuta-hime|ja|龍田姫|12=draft}}), is revered as a goddess of autumn and has been celebrated in many poems since ancient times. === Ranks of Deities === * 13th year of {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|12=draft}} (822), August 3: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." * 3rd year of {{ill|Kashō era|ja|嘉祥|12=draft}} (850), July 11: Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of {{ill|Ninju era|ja|仁寿|12=draft}} (852), July 25: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of Ninju (852), October 2: Junior Third Rank (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 3rd year of {{ill|Ten'an era|ja|天安|12=draft}} (859), January 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank to Senior Third Rank (from "{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. [https://kamenose.jp/] The shrine is also famous as a scenic spot for {{ill|autumn foliage|ja|紅葉|12=draft}}. The deity enshrined in the auxiliary shrine, Tatsuta Hime-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Tatsuta-hime|ja|龍田姫|12=draft}}), is revered as a goddess of autumn and has been celebrated in many poems since ancient times. === Ranks of Deities === * 13th year of {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|12=draft}} (822), August 3: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." * 3rd year of {{ill|Kashō era|ja|嘉祥|12=draft}} (850), July 11: Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of {{ill|Ninju era|ja|仁寿|12=draft}} (852), July 25: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of Ninju (852), October 2: Junior Third Rank (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 3rd year of {{ill|Ten'an era|ja|天安|12=draft}} (859), January 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank to Senior Third Rank (from "{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} | image = Tatsuta-Taisha03.JPG | caption = [[Torii]] at the entrance of Tatsuta Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|35|35|N|135|41|15|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]]
| founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = [[Kasuga-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sangō, Nara|Sangō]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||龍田神社|Tatsuta-jinja}}. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Overview== == Tatsuta Taisha == {{otheruses|Shrine in Sangō, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|Shrine in Ikaruga, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|{{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社|12=draft}}}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Tatsuta Taisha |image = [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}{{ill|Ikoma District|ja|生駒郡|12=draft}}{{ill|Sangō, Nara|ja|三郷町|12=draft}} Tateno Minami 1-chome 29-1 |ISO = JP-29 |lat_deg = 34|lat_min = 35|lat_sec = 35.1 |lon_deg = 135|lon_min = 41|lon_sec = 14.4 |deity = {{ill|Ame no Mihashira no Mikoto|ja|天御柱命|12=draft}}
{{ill|Kuni no Mihashira no Mikoto|ja|国御柱命|12=draft}} |rank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}} 2 seats)
{{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社|12=draft}} (Middle Seven Shrines)
Former {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft}}
{{ill|Beppyo Shrines|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} |established = Traditionally during the reign of the 10th {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|12=draft}} |main_hall = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造|12=draft}} |annual_festival = {{ill|April 4|ja|4月4日|12=draft}} |rituals = Taki Festival ({{ill|April 3|ja|4月3日|12=draft}})
Fūchin Grand Festival (1st Sunday of July)
Autumn Grand Festival (3rd Saturday and Sunday of October) |map = Japan Nara }} [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|{{center|{{ill|Torii|ja|鳥居|12=draft}}}}]] '''Tatsuta Taisha''' (龍田大社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in Tateno Minami, {{ill|Sangō, Nara|ja|三郷町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ikoma District|ja|生駒郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}}) and one of the {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社|12=draft}} (Middle Seven Shrines). Under the former shrine ranking system, it was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}}. It was formerly known as "Tatsuta Shrine." The shrine has long been venerated as the home of the deity of {{ill|Wind|ja|風|12=draft}} ({{ill|Fūjin|ja|風神|12=draft}}).[http://yamatoji.nara-kankou.or.jp/01shaji/01jinja/02west_area/tatsutataisha/] == History == == Overview == According to the "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}" {{ill|Norito|ja|祝詞|12=draft}} in the "Tatsuta Wind God Festival Norito," during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|12=draft}}, years of poor harvests and widespread epidemics prompted the emperor to personally pray to the heavenly and earthly deities. In a dream, he received a divine message instructing him to enshrine the two deities, {{ill|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|天御柱命|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|国御柱命|12=draft}}, on Mount Tatsuta, which led to the founding of the shrine. In the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|12=draft}}, the earliest record appears in the "{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}," stating that on April 10, the 4th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}} (675), an imperial envoy was dispatched to enshrine the Wind God at Tatsuta-Tateno and the Great Taboo God at Hirose Kawamuki. The "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}" {{ill|Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|Encho era|ja|延長|12=draft}} (927), lists "Tatsuta-no-Iwato-no-Ame-no-Mihashira-Kuni-no-Mihashira Jinja" with two deities in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Heguri District|ja|平群郡|12=draft}}. They are classified as {{ill|Myojin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}} and received offerings from the imperial court during the {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=draft}} and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭|12=draft}}. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft}} in 1871 (4th year of Meiji) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft}}. In 1948 (23rd year of Showa), it was included as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}}. In June 2020 (2nd year of Reiwa), Tatsuta Taisha, along with the {{ill|Tatsuta Ancient Road|ja|龍田古道|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kame-no-se|ja|亀の瀬|12=draft}}, was designated as a {{ill|Japan Heritage Site|ja|日本遺産|12=draft}}. [https://kamenose.jp/] The shrine is also famous as a scenic spot for {{ill|autumn foliage|ja|紅葉|12=draft}}. The deity enshrined in the auxiliary shrine, Tatsuta Hime-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Tatsuta-hime|ja|龍田姫|12=draft}}), is revered as a goddess of autumn and has been celebrated in many poems since ancient times. === Ranks of Deities === * 13th year of {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|12=draft}} (822), August 3: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." * 3rd year of {{ill|Kashō era|ja|嘉祥|12=draft}} (850), July 11: Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of {{ill|Ninju era|ja|仁寿|12=draft}} (852), July 25: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of Ninju (852), October 2: Junior Third Rank (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 3rd year of {{ill|Ten'an era|ja|天安|12=draft}} (859), January 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank to Senior Third Rank (from "{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} | image = Tatsuta-Taisha03.JPG | caption = [[Torii]] at the entrance of Tatsuta Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|35|35|N|135|41|15|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]]
| founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = [[Kasuga-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sangō, Nara|Sangō]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||龍田神社|Tatsuta-jinja}}. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Overview== == Tatsuta Taisha == {{otheruses|Shrine in Sangō, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|Shrine in Ikaruga, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|{{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社|12=draft}}}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Tatsuta Taisha |image = [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}{{ill|Ikoma District|ja|生駒郡|12=draft}}{{ill|Sangō, Nara|ja|三郷町|12=draft}} Tateno Minami 1-chome 29-1 |ISO = JP-29 |lat_deg = 34|lat_min = 35|lat_sec = 35.1 |lon_deg = 135|lon_min = 41|lon_sec = 14.4 |deity = {{ill|Ame no Mihashira no Mikoto|ja|天御柱命|12=draft}}
{{ill|Kuni no Mihashira no Mikoto|ja|国御柱命|12=draft}} |rank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}} 2 seats)
{{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社|12=draft}} (Middle Seven Shrines)
Former {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft}}
{{ill|Beppyo Shrines|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} |established = Traditionally during the reign of the 10th {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|12=draft}} |main_hall = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造|12=draft}} |annual_festival = {{ill|April 4|ja|4月4日|12=draft}} |rituals = Taki Festival ({{ill|April 3|ja|4月3日|12=draft}})
Fūchin Grand Festival (1st Sunday of July)
Autumn Grand Festival (3rd Saturday and Sunday of October) |map = Japan Nara }} [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|{{center|{{ill|Torii|ja|鳥居|12=draft}}}}]] '''Tatsuta Taisha''' (龍田大社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in Tateno Minami, {{ill|Sangō, Nara|ja|三郷町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ikoma District|ja|生駒郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}}) and one of the {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社|12=draft}} (Middle Seven Shrines). Under the former shrine ranking system, it was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}}. It was formerly known as "Tatsuta Shrine." The shrine has long been venerated as the home of the deity of {{ill|Wind|ja|風|12=draft}} ({{ill|Fūjin|ja|風神|12=draft}}).[http://yamatoji.nara-kankou.or.jp/01shaji/01jinja/02west_area/tatsutataisha/] == History == == Overview == According to the "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}" {{ill|Norito|ja|祝詞|12=draft}} in the "Tatsuta Wind God Festival Norito," during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|12=draft}}, years of poor harvests and widespread epidemics prompted the emperor to personally pray to the heavenly and earthly deities. In a dream, he received a divine message instructing him to enshrine the two deities, {{ill|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|天御柱命|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|国御柱命|12=draft}}, on Mount Tatsuta, which led to the founding of the shrine. In the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|12=draft}}, the earliest record appears in the "{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}," stating that on April 10, the 4th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}} (675), an imperial envoy was dispatched to enshrine the Wind God at Tatsuta-Tateno and the Great Taboo God at Hirose Kawamuki. The "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}" {{ill|Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|Encho era|ja|延長|12=draft}} (927), lists "Tatsuta-no-Iwato-no-Ame-no-Mihashira-Kuni-no-Mihashira Jinja" with two deities in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Heguri District|ja|平群郡|12=draft}}. They are classified as {{ill|Myojin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}} and received offerings from the imperial court during the {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=draft}} and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭|12=draft}}. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft}} in 1871 (4th year of Meiji) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft}}. In 1948 (23rd year of Showa), it was included as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}}. In June 2020 (2nd year of Reiwa), Tatsuta Taisha, along with the {{ill|Tatsuta Ancient Road|ja|龍田古道|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kame-no-se|ja|亀の瀬|12=draft}}, was designated as a {{ill|Japan Heritage Site|ja|日本遺産|12=draft}}. [https://kamenose.jp/] The shrine is also famous as a scenic spot for {{ill|autumn foliage|ja|紅葉|12=draft}}. The deity enshrined in the auxiliary shrine, Tatsuta Hime-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Tatsuta-hime|ja|龍田姫|12=draft}}), is revered as a goddess of autumn and has been celebrated in many poems since ancient times. === Ranks of Deities === * 13th year of {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|12=draft}} (822), August 3: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." * 3rd year of {{ill|Kashō era|ja|嘉祥|12=draft}} (850), July 11: Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of {{ill|Ninju era|ja|仁寿|12=draft}} (852), July 25: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of Ninju (852), October 2: Junior Third Rank (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 3rd year of {{ill|Ten'an era|ja|天安|12=draft}} (859), January 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank to Senior Third Rank (from "{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha of Yamato Province]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sangō, Nara]] [[Category:History of Sangō, Nara]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kasuga-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Tatsuta Taisha]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[ja:龍田大社]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]] [[sn:Tatsuta Taisha]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tatsuta Shrine}}

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} | image = Tatsuta-Taisha03.JPG | caption = [[Torii]] at the entrance of Tatsuta Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|35|35|N|135|41|15|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]]
| founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = [[Kasuga-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sangō, Nara|Sangō]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||龍田神社|Tatsuta-jinja}}. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Overview== == Tatsuta Taisha == {{otheruses|Shrine in Sangō, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|Shrine in Ikaruga, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|{{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社|12=draft}}}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Tatsuta Taisha |image = [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}{{ill|Ikoma District|ja|生駒郡|12=draft}}{{ill|Sangō, Nara|ja|三郷町|12=draft}} Tateno Minami 1-chome 29-1 |ISO = JP-29 |lat_deg = 34|lat_min = 35|lat_sec = 35.1 |lon_deg = 135|lon_min = 41|lon_sec = 14.4 |deity = {{ill|Ame no Mihashira no Mikoto|ja|天御柱命|12=draft}}
{{ill|Kuni no Mihashira no Mikoto|ja|国御柱命|12=draft}} |rank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}} 2 seats)
{{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社|12=draft}} (Middle Seven Shrines)
Former {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft}}
{{ill|Beppyo Shrines|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} |established = Traditionally during the reign of the 10th {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|12=draft}} |main_hall = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造|12=draft}} |annual_festival = {{ill|April 4|ja|4月4日|12=draft}} |rituals = Taki Festival ({{ill|April 3|ja|4月3日|12=draft}})
Fūchin Grand Festival (1st Sunday of July)
Autumn Grand Festival (3rd Saturday and Sunday of October) |map = Japan Nara }} [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|{{center|{{ill|Torii|ja|鳥居|12=draft}}}}]] '''Tatsuta Taisha''' (龍田大社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in Tateno Minami, {{ill|Sangō, Nara|ja|三郷町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ikoma District|ja|生駒郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|12=draft}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}}) and one of the {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社|12=draft}} (Middle Seven Shrines). Under the former shrine ranking system, it was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}}. It was formerly known as "Tatsuta Shrine." The shrine has long been venerated as the home of the deity of {{ill|Wind|ja|風|12=draft}} ({{ill|Fūjin|ja|風神|12=draft}}).[http://yamatoji.nara-kankou.or.jp/01shaji/01jinja/02west_area/tatsutataisha/] == History == == Overview == According to the "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}" {{ill|Norito|ja|祝詞|12=draft}} in the "Tatsuta Wind God Festival Norito," during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|12=draft}}, years of poor harvests and widespread epidemics prompted the emperor to personally pray to the heavenly and earthly deities. In a dream, he received a divine message instructing him to enshrine the two deities, {{ill|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|天御柱命|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|国御柱命|12=draft}}, on Mount Tatsuta, which led to the founding of the shrine. In the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|12=draft}}, the earliest record appears in the "{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}," stating that on April 10, the 4th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}} (675), an imperial envoy was dispatched to enshrine the Wind God at Tatsuta-Tateno and the Great Taboo God at Hirose Kawamuki. The "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}" {{ill|Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|Encho era|ja|延長|12=draft}} (927), lists "Tatsuta-no-Iwato-no-Ame-no-Mihashira-Kuni-no-Mihashira Jinja" with two deities in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Heguri District|ja|平群郡|12=draft}}. They are classified as {{ill|Myojin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}} and received offerings from the imperial court during the {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=draft}} and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭|12=draft}}. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft}} in 1871 (4th year of Meiji) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft}}. In 1948 (23rd year of Showa), it was included as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}}. In June 2020 (2nd year of Reiwa), Tatsuta Taisha, along with the {{ill|Tatsuta Ancient Road|ja|龍田古道|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kame-no-se|ja|亀の瀬|12=draft}}, was designated as a {{ill|Japan Heritage Site|ja|日本遺産|12=draft}}. [https://kamenose.jp/] The shrine is also famous as a scenic spot for {{ill|autumn foliage|ja|紅葉|12=draft}}. The deity enshrined in the auxiliary shrine, Tatsuta Hime-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Tatsuta-hime|ja|龍田姫|12=draft}}), is revered as a goddess of autumn and has been celebrated in many poems since ancient times. === Ranks of Deities === * 13th year of {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|12=draft}} (822), August 3: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." * 3rd year of {{ill|Kashō era|ja|嘉祥|12=draft}} (850), July 11: Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of {{ill|Ninju era|ja|仁寿|12=draft}} (852), July 25: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of Ninju (852), October 2: Junior Third Rank (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 3rd year of {{ill|Ten'an era|ja|天安|12=draft}} (859), January 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank to Senior Third Rank (from "{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha of Yamato Province]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sangō, Nara]] [[Category:History of Sangō, Nara]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kasuga-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Tatsuta Taisha]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:龍田大社]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]] [[sn:Tatsuta Taisha]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tatsuta Shrine}}

[https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Overview== == Tatsuta Taisha == {{otheruses|Shrine in Sangō, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|Shrine in Ikaruga, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|{{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社|12=draft}}}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Tatsuta Taisha |image = [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, haiden.jpg|270px]]
Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=270}} |location = {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}{{ill|Ikoma District|ja|生駒郡|12=draft}}{{ill|Sangō, Nara|ja|三郷町|12=draft}} Tateno Minami 1-chome 29-1 |ISO = JP-29 |lat_deg = 34|lat_min = 35|lat_sec = 35.1 |lon_deg = 135|lon_min = 41|lon_sec = 14.4 |deity = {{ill|Ame no Mihashira no Mikoto|ja|天御柱命|12=draft}}
{{ill|Kuni no Mihashira no Mikoto|ja|国御柱命|12=draft}} |rank = {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}} 2 seats)
{{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社|12=draft}} (Middle Seven Shrines)
Former {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft}}
{{ill|Beppyo Shrines|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} |established = Traditionally during the reign of the 10th {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|12=draft}} |main_hall = {{ill|Kasuga-zukuri|ja|春日造|12=draft}} |annual_festival = {{ill|April 4|ja|4月4日|12=draft}} |rituals = Taki Festival ({{ill|April 3|ja|4月3日|12=draft}})
Fūchin Grand Festival (1st Sunday of July)
Autumn Grand Festival (3rd Saturday and Sunday of October) |map = Japan Nara }} [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|{{center|{{ill|Torii|ja|鳥居|12=draft}}}}]] '''Tatsuta Taisha''' (龍田大社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in Tateno Minami, {{ill|Sangō, Nara|ja|三郷町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ikoma District|ja|生駒郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}}) and one of the {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社|12=draft}} (Middle Seven Shrines). Under the former shrine ranking system, it was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}}. It was formerly known as "Tatsuta Shrine." The shrine has long been venerated as the home of the deity of {{ill|Wind|ja|風|12=draft}} ({{ill|Fūjin|ja|風神|12=draft}}).[http://yamatoji.nara-kankou.or.jp/01shaji/01jinja/02west_area/tatsutataisha/] == History == == Overview == According to the "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}" {{ill|Norito|ja|祝詞|12=draft}} in the "Tatsuta Wind God Festival Norito," during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|12=draft}}, years of poor harvests and widespread epidemics prompted the emperor to personally pray to the heavenly and earthly deities. In a dream, he received a divine message instructing him to enshrine the two deities, {{ill|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|天御柱命|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|国御柱命|12=draft}}, on Mount Tatsuta, which led to the founding of the shrine. In the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|12=draft}}, the earliest record appears in the "{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}," stating that on April 10, the 4th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}} (675), an imperial envoy was dispatched to enshrine the Wind God at Tatsuta-Tateno and the Great Taboo God at Hirose Kawamuki. The "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}" {{ill|Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|Encho era|ja|延長|12=draft}} (927), lists "Tatsuta-no-Iwato-no-Ame-no-Mihashira-Kuni-no-Mihashira Jinja" with two deities in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Heguri District|ja|平群郡|12=draft}}. They are classified as {{ill|Myojin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}} and received offerings from the imperial court during the {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=draft}} and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭|12=draft}}. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft}} in 1871 (4th year of Meiji) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft}}. In 1948 (23rd year of Showa), it was included as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}}. In June 2020 (2nd year of Reiwa), Tatsuta Taisha, along with the {{ill|Tatsuta Ancient Road|ja|龍田古道|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kame-no-se|ja|亀の瀬|12=draft}}, was designated as a {{ill|Japan Heritage Site|ja|日本遺産|12=draft}}. [https://kamenose.jp/] The shrine is also famous as a scenic spot for {{ill|autumn foliage|ja|紅葉|12=draft}}. The deity enshrined in the auxiliary shrine, Tatsuta Hime-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Tatsuta-hime|ja|龍田姫|12=draft}}), is revered as a goddess of autumn and has been celebrated in many poems since ancient times. === Ranks of Deities === * 13th year of {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|12=draft}} (822), August 3: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." * 3rd year of {{ill|Kashō era|ja|嘉祥|12=draft}} (850), July 11: Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of {{ill|Ninju era|ja|仁寿|12=draft}} (852), July 25: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of Ninju (852), October 2: Junior Third Rank (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 3rd year of {{ill|Ten'an era|ja|天安|12=draft}} (859), January 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank to Senior Third Rank (from "{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. Fūchin Grand Festival (1st Sunday of July)
Autumn Grand Festival (3rd Saturday and Sunday of October) |map = Japan Nara }} [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|{{center|{{ill|Torii|ja|鳥居|12=draft}}}}]] '''Tatsuta Taisha''' (龍田大社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in Tateno Minami, {{ill|Sangō, Nara|ja|三郷町|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ikoma District|ja|生駒郡|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft}}. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}}) and one of the {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社|12=draft}} (Middle Seven Shrines). Under the former shrine ranking system, it was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}}. It was formerly known as "Tatsuta Shrine." The shrine has long been venerated as the home of the deity of {{ill|Wind|ja|風|12=draft}} ({{ill|Fūjin|ja|風神|12=draft}}).[http://yamatoji.nara-kankou.or.jp/01shaji/01jinja/02west_area/tatsutataisha/] == History == == Overview == According to the "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}" {{ill|Norito|ja|祝詞|12=draft}} in the "Tatsuta Wind God Festival Norito," during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|12=draft}}, years of poor harvests and widespread epidemics prompted the emperor to personally pray to the heavenly and earthly deities. In a dream, he received a divine message instructing him to enshrine the two deities, {{ill|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|天御柱命|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|国御柱命|12=draft}}, on Mount Tatsuta, which led to the founding of the shrine. In the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|12=draft}}, the earliest record appears in the "{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}," stating that on April 10, the 4th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}} (675), an imperial envoy was dispatched to enshrine the Wind God at Tatsuta-Tateno and the Great Taboo God at Hirose Kawamuki. The "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}}" {{ill|Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|Encho era|ja|延長|12=draft}} (927), lists "Tatsuta-no-Iwato-no-Ame-no-Mihashira-Kuni-no-Mihashira Jinja" with two deities in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Heguri District|ja|平群郡|12=draft}}. They are classified as {{ill|Myojin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft}} and received offerings from the imperial court during the {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=draft}} and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭|12=draft}}. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft}} in 1871 (4th year of Meiji) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft}}. In 1948 (23rd year of Showa), it was included as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}}. In June 2020 (2nd year of Reiwa), Tatsuta Taisha, along with the {{ill|Tatsuta Ancient Road|ja|龍田古道|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kame-no-se|ja|亀の瀬|12=draft}}, was designated as a {{ill|Japan Heritage Site|ja|日本遺産|12=draft}}. [https://kamenose.jp/] The shrine is also famous as a scenic spot for {{ill|autumn foliage|ja|紅葉|12=draft}}. The deity enshrined in the auxiliary shrine, Tatsuta Hime-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Tatsuta-hime|ja|龍田姫|12=draft}}), is revered as a goddess of autumn and has been celebrated in many poems since ancient times. === Ranks of Deities === * 13th year of {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|12=draft}} (822), August 3: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." * 3rd year of {{ill|Kashō era|ja|嘉祥|12=draft}} (850), July 11: Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of {{ill|Ninju era|ja|仁寿|12=draft}} (852), July 25: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of Ninju (852), October 2: Junior Third Rank (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 3rd year of {{ill|Ten'an era|ja|天安|12=draft}} (859), January 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank to Senior Third Rank (from "{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha of Yamato Province]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sangō, Nara]] [[Category:History of Sangō, Nara]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kasuga-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Tatsuta Taisha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tatsuta Taisha]][[Category:qq]] }} [[ja:龍田大社]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]] [[sn:Tatsuta Taisha]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tatsuta Shrine}} [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} | image = Tatsuta-Taisha03.JPG | caption = [[Torii]] at the entrance of Tatsuta Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|35|35|N|135|41|15|E|source:jawiki_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Shinatsuhiko]]
| founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = [[Kasuga-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Tatsuta Shrine'''|龍田大社|Tatsuta-taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Sangō, Nara|Sangō]], [[Nara Prefecture|Nara]] in [[Japan]]. The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||龍田神社|Tatsuta-jinja}}. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Overview== == Tatsuta Taisha == {{otheruses|Shrine in Sangō, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|Shrine in Ikaruga, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|{{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社|12=draft|12=draft}}}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Tatsuta Taisha |image = [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, haiden.jpg|270px]]

It was formerly known as "Tatsuta Shrine." The shrine has long been venerated as the home of the deity of {{ill|Wind|ja|風|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Fūjin|ja|風神|12=draft|12=draft}}).[http://yamatoji.nara-kankou.or.jp/01shaji/01jinja/02west_area/tatsutataisha/] == History == == Overview == According to the "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft|12=draft}}" {{ill|Norito|ja|祝詞|12=draft|12=draft}} in the "Tatsuta Wind God Festival Norito," during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}, years of poor harvests and widespread epidemics prompted the emperor to personally pray to the heavenly and earthly deities. In a dream, he received a divine message instructing him to enshrine the two deities, {{ill|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|天御柱命|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|国御柱命|12=draft|12=draft}}, on Mount Tatsuta, which led to the founding of the shrine. In the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|12=draft|12=draft}}, the earliest record appears in the "{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}," stating that on April 10, the 4th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft|12=draft}} (675), an imperial envoy was dispatched to enshrine the Wind God at Tatsuta-Tateno and the Great Taboo God at Hirose Kawamuki. The "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft|12=draft}}" {{ill|Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft|12=draft}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|Encho era|ja|延長|12=draft|12=draft}} (927), lists "Tatsuta-no-Iwato-no-Ame-no-Mihashira-Kuni-no-Mihashira Jinja" with two deities in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Heguri District|ja|平群郡|12=draft|12=draft}}. They are classified as {{ill|Myojin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft|12=draft}} and received offerings from the imperial court during the {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭|12=draft|12=draft}}. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft|12=draft}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1871 (4th year of Meiji) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft|12=draft}}. In 1948 (23rd year of Showa), it was included as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft|12=draft}}. In June 2020 (2nd year of Reiwa), Tatsuta Taisha, along with the {{ill|Tatsuta Ancient Road|ja|龍田古道|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kame-no-se|ja|亀の瀬|12=draft|12=draft}}, was designated as a {{ill|Japan Heritage Site|ja|日本遺産|12=draft|12=draft}}. [https://kamenose.jp/] The shrine is also famous as a scenic spot for {{ill|autumn foliage|ja|紅葉|12=draft|12=draft}}. The deity enshrined in the auxiliary shrine, Tatsuta Hime-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Tatsuta-hime|ja|龍田姫|12=draft|12=draft}}), is revered as a goddess of autumn and has been celebrated in many poems since ancient times. === Ranks of Deities === * 13th year of {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|12=draft|12=draft}} (822), August 3: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略|12=draft|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." * 3rd year of {{ill|Kashō era|ja|嘉祥|12=draft|12=draft}} (850), July 11: Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録|12=draft|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of {{ill|Ninju era|ja|仁寿|12=draft|12=draft}} (852), July 25: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of Ninju (852), October 2: Junior Third Rank (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 3rd year of {{ill|Ten'an era|ja|天安|12=draft|12=draft}} (859), January 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank to Senior Third Rank (from "{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. Autumn Grand Festival (3rd Saturday and Sunday of October) |map = Japan Nara }} [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, torii.jpg|thumb|220px|{{center|{{ill|Torii|ja|鳥居|12=draft|12=draft}}}}]] '''Tatsuta Taisha''' (龍田大社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} located in Tateno Minami, {{ill|Sangō, Nara|ja|三郷町|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ikoma District|ja|生駒郡|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県|12=draft|12=draft}}. It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft|12=draft}}) and one of the {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社|12=draft|12=draft}} (Middle Seven Shrines). Under the former shrine Ranking system, it was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft|12=draft}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft|12=draft}}. It was formerly known as "Tatsuta Shrine." The shrine has long been venerated as the home of the deity of {{ill|Wind|ja|風|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Fūjin|ja|風神|12=draft|12=draft}}).[http://yamatoji.nara-kankou.or.jp/01shaji/01jinja/02west_area/tatsutataisha/] == History == == Overview == According to the "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft|12=draft}}" {{ill|Norito|ja|祝詞|12=draft|12=draft}} in the "Tatsuta Wind God Festival Norito," during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}, years of poor harvests and widespread epidemics prompted the emperor to personally pray to the heavenly and earthly deities. In a dream, he received a divine message instructing him to enshrine the two deities, {{ill|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|天御柱命|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|国御柱命|12=draft|12=draft}}, on Mount Tatsuta, which led to the founding of the shrine. In the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|12=draft|12=draft}}, the earliest record appears in the "{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}," stating that on April 10, the 4th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft|12=draft}} (675), an imperial envoy was dispatched to enshrine the Wind God at Tatsuta-Tateno and the Great Taboo God at Hirose Kawamuki. The "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft|12=draft}}" {{ill|Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft|12=draft}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|Encho era|ja|延長|12=draft|12=draft}} (927), lists "Tatsuta-no-Iwato-no-Ame-no-Mihashira-Kuni-no-Mihashira Jinja" with two deities in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Heguri District|ja|平群郡|12=draft|12=draft}}. They are classified as {{ill|Myojin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft|12=draft}} and received offerings from the imperial court during the {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭|12=draft|12=draft}}. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft|12=draft}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1871 (4th year of Meiji) under the {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft|12=draft}}. In 1948 (23rd year of Showa), it was included as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft|12=draft}}. In June 2020 (2nd year of Reiwa), Tatsuta Taisha, along with the {{ill|Tatsuta Ancient Road|ja|龍田古道|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kame-no-se|ja|亀の瀬|12=draft|12=draft}}, was designated as a {{ill|Japan Heritage Site|ja|日本遺産|12=draft|12=draft}}. [https://kamenose.jp/] The shrine is also famous as a scenic spot for {{ill|autumn foliage|ja|紅葉|12=draft|12=draft}}. The deity enshrined in the auxiliary shrine, Tatsuta Hime-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Tatsuta-hime|ja|龍田姫|12=draft|12=draft}}), is revered as a goddess of autumn and has been celebrated in many poems since ancient times. === Ranks of Deities === * 13th year of {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|12=draft|12=draft}} (822), August 3: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略|12=draft|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." * 3rd year of {{ill|Kashō era|ja|嘉祥|12=draft|12=draft}} (850), July 11: Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録|12=draft|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of {{ill|Ninju era|ja|仁寿|12=draft|12=draft}} (852), July 25: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of Ninju (852), October 2: Junior Third Rank (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 3rd year of {{ill|Ten'an era|ja|天安|12=draft|12=draft}} (859), January 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank to Senior Third Rank (from "{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] ]] ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinai-sha of Yamato Province]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Sangō, Nara]] [[Category:History of Sangō, Nara]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kasuga-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Tatsuta Taisha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Tatsuta Taisha]] [[ja:龍田大社]] [[en:Tatsuta Taisha]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tatsuta Shrine}}

The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||龍田神社|Tatsuta-jinja}}. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, [[Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Tatsuta Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Overview== == Tatsuta Taisha == {{otheruses|Shrine in Sangō, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|Shrine in Ikaruga, Ikoma District, Nara Prefecture|{{ill|Tatsuta Shrine|ja|龍田神社|12=draft|12=draft}}}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Tatsuta Taisha |image = [[File:Tatsuta-taisha, haiden.jpg|270px]]

It is listed as a {{ill|Shikinai-sha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Myōjin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft|12=draft}}) and one of the {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|ja|二十二社|12=draft|12=draft}} (Middle Seven Shrines). Under the former shrine Ranking system, it was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft|12=draft}}, and it is currently listed as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft|12=draft}} by the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft|12=draft}}. It was formerly known as "Tatsuta Shrine." The shrine has long been venerated as the home of the deity of {{ill|Wind|ja|風|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Fūjin|ja|風神|12=draft|12=draft}}).[http://yamatoji.nara-kankou.or.jp/01shaji/01jinja/02west_area/tatsutataisha/] == History == == Overview == According to the "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft|12=draft}}" {{ill|Norito|ja|祝詞|12=draft|12=draft}} in the "Tatsuta Wind God Festival Norito," during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}, years of poor harvests and widespread epidemics prompted the emperor to personally pray to the heavenly and earthly deities. In a dream, he received a divine message instructing him to enshrine the two deities, {{ill|Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|天御柱命|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto|ja|国御柱命|12=draft|12=draft}}, on Mount Tatsuta, which led to the founding of the shrine. In the {{ill|Six National Histories|ja|六国史|12=draft|12=draft}}, the earliest record appears in the "{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}," stating that on April 10, the 4th year of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft|12=draft}} (675), an imperial envoy was dispatched to enshrine the Wind God at Tatsuta-Tateno and the Great Taboo God at Hirose Kawamuki. The "{{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft|12=draft}}" {{ill|Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=draft|12=draft}}, compiled in the 5th year of {{ill|Encho era|ja|延長|12=draft|12=draft}} (927), lists "Tatsuta-no-Iwato-no-Ame-no-Mihashira-Kuni-no-Mihashira Jinja" with two deities in {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|Yamato Province|12=draft|12=draft}}'s {{ill|Heguri District|ja|平群郡|12=draft|12=draft}}. They are classified as {{ill|Myojin Taisha|ja|名神大社|fr|Myōjin Taisha|zh|名神大社|12=draft|12=draft}} and received offerings from the imperial court during the {{ill|Tsukinami-sai|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Niiname-sai|ja|新嘗祭|12=draft|12=draft}}. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft|12=draft}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Kanpei Taisha|ja|官幣大社|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1871 (4th year of Meiji) under the {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft|12=draft}}. In 1948 (23rd year of Showa), it was included as a {{ill|Beppyo Shrine|ja|別表神社|12=draft|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft|12=draft}}. In June 2020 (2nd year of Reiwa), Tatsuta Taisha, along with the {{ill|Tatsuta Ancient Road|ja|龍田古道|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Kame-no-se|ja|亀の瀬|12=draft|12=draft}}, was designated as a {{ill|Japan Heritage Site|ja|日本遺産|12=draft|12=draft}}. [https://kamenose.jp/] The shrine is also famous as a scenic spot for {{ill|autumn foliage|ja|紅葉|12=draft|12=draft}}. The deity enshrined in the auxiliary shrine, Tatsuta Hime-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Tatsuta-hime|ja|龍田姫|12=draft|12=draft}}), is revered as a goddess of autumn and has been celebrated in many poems since ancient times. === Ranks of Deities === * 13th year of {{ill|Kōnin era|ja|弘仁|12=draft|12=draft}} (822), August 3: Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Kiryaku|ja|日本紀略|12=draft|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." * 3rd year of {{ill|Kashō era|ja|嘉祥|12=draft|12=draft}} (850), July 11: Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade (from "{{ill|Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku|ja|日本文徳天皇実録|12=draft|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of {{ill|Ninju era|ja|仁寿|12=draft|12=draft}} (852), July 25: Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 2nd year of Ninju (852), October 2: Junior Third Rank (from "Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku") - Referred to as "Ame-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto" and "Kuni-no-Mihashira-no-Mikoto." * 3rd year of {{ill|Ten'an era|ja|天安|12=draft|12=draft}} (859), January 27: Promoted from Junior Third Rank to Senior Third Rank (from "{{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録|12=draft|12=draft}}") - Referred to as "Tatsuta no Kami." == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Twenty-Two Shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Tatsuta Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]].
